description:
You are a compliance officer who must produce a very small, machine-verifiable inventory of the organisation’s local certificates.

Current situation
1. Two PEM-encoded certificate files already exist in
   /home/user/company_certs/
       ├── alpha.crt
       └── bravo.crt
   (You do NOT need to create or modify these files.)

2. No compliance log directory exists yet.

What you must deliver
Create the directory /home/user/compliance_logs/ (if it does not yet exist) and inside it create a new file named
   /home/user/compliance_logs/certificate_inventory.log

The file must follow this exact CSV specification so that the automated audit program can parse it:

• Line-1 (header) must be the literal text, including lower-case spelling and the two commas:
        filename,sha256,size_bytes

• Line-2 → Line-n  
  For every *.crt file located in /home/user/company_certs/ write one line containing three comma-separated fields in this order:
        1. The certificate’s base name (e.g. alpha.crt)  
        2. The SHA-256 checksum of the file expressed as 64 lowercase hexadecimal characters (the exact output produced by “sha256sum” without the trailing filename)  
        3. The certificate’s file size in bytes (the integer shown by “stat -c%s <file>”)  

Additional format rules
a. Do NOT put extra spaces anywhere in the line.  
b. Do NOT surround fields with quotes.  
c. The lines MUST be sorted alphabetically by the filename field.  
d. The file must end with a single trailing newline character.  

After creating the log, display its entire contents to the terminal so the auditor can see the result.

Permissions
The log file itself must be created with default user permissions (no sudo is allowed).

Example (illustrative only – the actual hashes and sizes will be different):
filename,sha256,size_bytes
alpha.crt,3a2286c8e690a647db0b0bef66240cf2d76a0d1ba9a4e8e0b6d8d26f06ea9f1c,1244
bravo.crt,b1766504a87ac9b5aec1c009938f0ad61df6ebadc177a64e1242d5e6f46f98d5,1190

To complete the task you will probably need just three or four standard GNU/Linux commands such as mkdir, sha256sum, stat, sort, and some shell redirection.

When everything looks correct, remember to cat (or otherwise output) the whole /home/user/compliance_logs/certificate_inventory.log to the console.

Initial state prepared for the agent
1. The directory /home/user/company_certs/ exists with two certificate files:

   /home/user/company_certs/alpha.crt
   Contents (exact, including newlines):
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIBrzCCAVSgAwIBAgIUBEHPxfN3E3xaQa58aeGeq/QAdzMwCgYIKoZIzj0EAwIw
EzERMA8GA1UEAwwIYWxwaGEuY29tMB4XDTIzMDgwMTAwMDAwMFoXDTI0MDgwMTAw

EwEB/wQFMAMBAf8wCgYIKoZIzj0EAwIDSQAwRgIhAKZDdp2PbgM3N3aR6Vrn956x
BY2NU4VFIfE88ll/aT0cAiEA+2CevulaHYodEc/WWEDuJeayQMZT6X0hgqP/d9vy
q6A=
-----END CERTIFICATE-----

   /home/user/company_certs/bravo.crt
   Contents (exact, including newlines):
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIBqTCCAU+gAwIBAgIUYr2vNhbbX6YsJhBKt3vnDnN/SfAwCgYIKoZIzj0EAwIw
FDESMBAGA1UEAwwJYnJhdm8uY29tMB4XDTIzMDgwMTAwMDAwMFoXDTI0MDgwMTAw
MDAwMFowFDESMBAGA1UEAwwJYnJhdm8uY29tMFkwEwYHKoZIzj0CAQYIKoZIzj0D
AQcDQgAEcJsteUms9UpAJZciV06P88GJEqn3Ejj6inUeJ8V+RaHcRUW2KIiMzFxL
0X58F3RDeo63eVUsVTNff7kwh28zd6NTMFEwHQYDVR0OBBYEFFIqhzyapMI2vlA9
SxrdbidKfnUSMB8GA1UdIwQYMBaAFFIqhzyapMI2vlA9SxrdbidKfnUSMA8GA1Ud
EwEB/wQFMAMBAf8wCgYIKoZIzj0EAwIDRwAwRAIgUoil58x2oyS9MhUlCT3VkOIT

xup6
-----END CERTIFICATE-----

2. No other *.crt files are present anywhere beneath /home/user/company_certs/.

3. The directory /home/user/compliance_logs/ does NOT exist at start-up.

What the test suite will verify AFTER the agent finishes
A. /home/user/compliance_logs/certificate_inventory.log exists.  
B. First line is literally: filename,sha256,size_bytes  
C. Exactly two further lines exist, sorted alphabetically:
      line-2 begins “alpha.crt,”
      line-3 begins “bravo.crt,”  
D. Each of those lines contains three comma-separated fields:
      field-2 matches the 64-hex-character regex [0-9a-f]{64}  
      field-3 matches regex [1-9][0-9]* and equals the actual byte-size of the file.  
E. The test script will compute the SHA-256 of each file itself and compare to the value present in the log.  
F. The file ends with a trailing newline (checked via POSIX wc -l).  

Because the test recomputes the checksums and file sizes, the exact numeric values are not hard-coded here. Only file presence, naming, ordering and formatting are enforced.
